 {"id":"wa-1ez8","title":"Set up automated nightly rebase CI for WezTerm fork","description":"## Overview

WezTerm is updated nightly by its author. To maintain our feature branch, we need automated rebasing with conflict detection and alerting.

## Prerequisites

- Completed: wa-20fw (WezTerm fork with wa integration)

## CI Workflow Design

### GitHub Actions Workflow

\`\`\`.github/workflows/rebase-upstream.yml\`\`\`
\`\`\`yaml
name: Rebase on Upstream WezTerm

on:
  schedule:
    - cron: '0 6 * * *'  # Daily at 6 AM UTC
  workflow_dispatch:      # Manual trigger

jobs:
  rebase:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with:
          fetch-depth: 0
          ref: feature/wa-integration
          token: \${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
      
      - name: Configure Git
        run: |
          git config user.name "wa-bot"
          git config user.email "wa-bot@example.com"
      
      - name: Add Upstream Remote
        run: |
          git remote add upstream https://github.com/wez/wezterm.git
          git fetch upstream main
      
      - name: Attempt Rebase
        id: rebase
        continue-on-error: true
        run: |
          git rebase upstream/main
          echo "rebase_status=\$?" >> \$GITHUB_OUTPUT
      
      - name: Push if Successful
        if: steps.rebase.outcome == 'success'
        run: |
          git push --force-with-lease origin feature/wa-integration
      
      - name: Create Conflict Issue
        if: steps.rebase.outcome == 'failure'
        uses: actions/github-script@v7
        with:
          script: |
            const { data: issues } = await github.rest.issues.listForRepo({
              owner: context.repo.owner,
              repo: context.repo.repo,
              labels: 'rebase-conflict',
              state: 'open'
            });
            
            if (issues.length === 0) {
              await github.rest.issues.create({
                owner: context.repo.owner,
                repo: context.repo.repo,
                title: 'Rebase conflict with upstream WezTerm',
                body: \`The nightly rebase on upstream WezTerm failed due to conflicts.
                
                **Action Required**: Manual resolution needed.
                
                \\\`\\\`\\\`bash
                git fetch upstream main
                git rebase upstream/main
                # Resolve conflicts
                git rebase --continue
                git push --force-with-lease
                \\\`\\\`\\\`
                \`,
                labels: ['rebase-conflict', 'urgent']
              });
            }
      
      - name: Run Tests After Rebase
        if: steps.rebase.outcome == 'success'
        run: |
          cargo build --features wa-integration
          cargo test --features wa-integration

  notify:
    needs: rebase
    if: failure()
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Send Notification
        run: |
          # Notify via email, Slack, Discord, etc.
          echo "Rebase failed - notification would be sent"
\`\`\`

### Handling Conflicts

When conflicts occur:
1. CI creates GitHub issue with "rebase-conflict" label
2. Human/agent reviews conflicting files
3. Usually conflicts are in files we modified (minimal set)
4. Resolve, test, push

### Minimizing Conflicts

To reduce conflict frequency:
1. Keep wa changes behind \`#[cfg(feature)]\` — no logic changes to upstream code
2. Use separate files where possible (mux/src/wa_events.rs)
3. Add wa code at end of functions, not middle
4. Avoid reformatting upstream code

## Alternative: Patch-Based Approach

Instead of maintaining a branch, maintain a patch series:

\`\`\`bash
# Generate patches
git format-patch upstream/main..feature/wa-integration -o patches/

# Apply patches to fresh clone
git clone --depth=1 https://github.com/wez/wezterm.git
cd wezterm
git am ../patches/*.patch
\`\`\`

Patches are more explicit about what we change, easier to review.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] GitHub Actions workflow created
- [ ] Nightly rebase runs automatically
- [ ] Conflicts create GitHub issues
- [ ] Notifications configured
- [ ] Post-rebase tests run
- [ ] Documentation for manual conflict resolution
- [ ] 30-day trial period: track conflict frequency

## Files to Create

- .github/workflows/rebase-upstream.yml
- docs/maintaining-wezterm-fork.md

## References

- GitHub Actions: https://docs.github.com/en/actions
- Git rebase: https://git-scm.com/docs/git-rebase

-{"id":"wa-2t2","title":"Layout tree capture — window/tab/pane hierarchy with split topology","description":"## Goal
Capture the complete window -> tab -> pane layout hierarchy including split topology (horizontal/vertical splits with ratios) from the running mux server.

## Background
WezTerm organizes panes in a tree structure: Windows contain Tabs, Tabs contain a tree of Panes split horizontally or vertically. The resurrect.wezterm plugin captures this via Lua, but we need to capture it programmatically via the mux protocol or CLI.

The wezterm cli list command provides flat pane info (pane_id, tab_id, window_id, workspace, title, cwd, size). But it does NOT provide split topology information. To capture the full tree, we need either:
a) The vendored mux client with appropriate PDUs
b) A Lua script executed via wezterm cli (wezterm lua)
c) Parsing from wezterm cli list-clients or other commands

## Design
1. Primary approach: Use wezterm's Lua API via `wezterm cli spawn --` or custom Lua:
   ```lua
   -- Enumerate full layout tree
   for _, window in ipairs(wezterm.mux.all_windows()) do
     for _, tab in ipairs(window:tabs()) do
       local panes_with_info = tab:panes_with_info()
       -- panes_with_info includes: pane, is_active, left, top, width, height, pixel_width, pixel_height, index
     end
   end
   ```

2. Fallback: Parse flat pane list + infer splits from position/size data
   - wezterm cli list provides pane dimensions (left, top, width, height)
   - Can reconstruct the split tree from spatial relationships
   - Less reliable but doesn't require Lua execution

3. Capture workspace assignments for each window
4. Capture active tab per window, active pane per tab
5. Build the recursive PaneNode tree from the layout data

## Key Consideration
The panes_with_info() Lua method returns positional data that can be used to reconstruct the split tree. Each pane has left/top/width/height in cells. Adjacent panes sharing an edge at the same position indicate a split boundary.

## Algorithm for tree reconstruction from flat position data:
1. Start with all panes in a tab
2. Find the split axis (vertical or horizontal) by checking if panes can be partitioned into two groups along x or y axis
3. Recursively build subtrees for each partition
4. Compute split ratio from the size proportions

## Dependencies
- Requires MuxSnapshot schema (bead 1) for WindowSnapshot, TabSnapshot, PaneNode

## Acceptance Criteria
- Correctly captures split topology for: single pane, 2-way split, 3-way split, nested splits
- Captures active tab/pane state
- Handles workspaces
- Works via both Lua approach and fallback position-inference approach
- Roundtrip test: capture layout, serialize, deserialize, verify matches original

## Benchmark & Property Testing Requirements

### Proptest
Add `tests/proptest_layout_tree.rs`:
- **tree_serialization_roundtrip**: For any arbitrary PaneNode tree (proptest generates random trees with 1-20 leaves, random HSplit/VSplit nesting, random ratios 0.1-0.9), assert that serde_json::from_str(serde_json::to_string(tree)) == tree. This validates that no layout topology is lost during serialization.
- **tree_from_positions_roundtrip**: For any valid PaneNode tree, compute the flat position list (left, top, width, height for each leaf), then reconstruct the tree from positions. Assert the reconstructed tree is structurally equivalent to the original.
- **split_ratio_preservation**: For any tree with arbitrary split ratios, assert that ratios survive roundtrip within f64 epsilon tolerance.

### Criterion Benchmarks
Add `benches/layout_tree.rs` using criterion:
- **tree_traversal_depth**: Benchmark traversal (collect all leaf PaneSnapshots) for trees of depth 2, 4, 8, 16. Target: <10us for depth 8 (typical max nesting).
- **tree_reconstruction_from_positions**: Benchmark the algorithm that reconstructs PaneNode tree from flat pane position data, for 2, 5, 10, 20 panes.
- **tree_serialization**: Benchmark serde_json serialization/deserialization for trees with 5, 20, 50 leaf panes.

## Cross-References
- **wa-e6pq** (Layout restoration engine): wa-2t2 captures the layout tree; wa-e6pq consumes it to recreate the physical window/tab/split topology. The PaneNode tree structure defined here is the contract between capture and restore. Any changes to the tree representation must be coordinated between both beads.
- **wa-2dd4s.3** (Swap layouts): FrankenTerm's swap-layout feature allows users to switch between saved layout configurations. bd-2t2's layout tree capture provides the serialized format that swap-layouts will store and retrieve. Ensure the PaneNode schema supports metadata fields (e.g., layout name, tags) needed by the swap-layout system.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-02-09T19:20:32.139691Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-10T19:49:01.301338Z"}

 {"id":"wa-2t4oa","title":"Implement structured config format (TOML + WASM config evaluation)","description":"# Implement Structured Config Format (TOML + WASM Config Evaluation)

## Context (revised from original "native Rust config loader")
Under the dual-runtime strategy, we need a config system that supports THREE modes:
1. **wezterm.lua** — backward-compatible Lua config (when `lua` feature enabled)
2. **frankenterm.toml** — structured TOML config (always available, no scripting engine needed)
3. **frankenterm.wasm** — WASM-evaluated config (when `wasm` feature enabled, for dynamic configs)

## What to build

### frankenterm.toml schema
Define a TOML config format covering the WezTerm config subset FrankenTerm uses:
- Terminal settings: scrollback_lines, cursor_style, cursor_blink_rate
- SSH/mux connection settings: ssh_domains, unix_domains, tls_domains
- Color schemes: base16 compatible, loadable from JSON
- Key bindings: action-based (subset of WezTerm key_tables)
- Font settings: font_family, font_size, freetype_load_target
- Extension settings: enabled_extensions, extension_search_paths

### Config loading priority
1. CLI flags (highest priority)
2. Environment variables (WA_*, FRANKENTERM_*)
3. frankenterm.toml / wezterm.lua / frankenterm.wasm (whichever found)
4. Built-in defaults (lowest priority)

### WASM config evaluation
When a .wasm config is detected and `wasm` feature is enabled:
- Load the WASM module via wasmtime
- Call its `configure()` export which returns structured config
- The WASM module can access env vars, hostname, OS info via WASI
- This allows dynamic config (like Lua) but sandboxed and in any language

## Design principle
The config format should be **agent-friendly**: structured, predictable, introspectable. AI agents shouldn't need to generate Lua code to configure the terminal. TOML is the default for simplicity; WASM is for power users who need dynamic logic.

## Files affected
- frankenterm/config/src/lib.rs (config detection, multi-format loading)
- frankenterm/config/src/toml_config.rs (NEW — TOML parser)
- frankenterm/config/src/wasm_config.rs (NEW — WASM config evaluator)
- frankenterm/config/src/defaults.rs (built-in defaults)

## Testing
- Load a frankenterm.toml and verify all fields parse correctly
- Load a wezterm.lua (with lua feature) and verify backward compat
- Load a frankenterm.wasm and verify dynamic config evaluation
- Test priority resolution (CLI > env > file > defaults)
- Test error handling: missing file, malformed TOML, WASM panic, etc.

## Test Framework Requirements
- **Proptest**: Property-based tests for TOML parsing roundtrips:
  - Generate arbitrary config structs, serialize to TOML, deserialize back, and verify equality (roundtrip identity)

 {"id":"wa-2tk","title":"Metrics export: Prometheus endpoint with counters, gauges, histograms","description":"# Metrics Export

## Purpose
Expose Prometheus-compatible metrics for monitoring and alerting.

## Implementation

### Metrics Registry
```rust
use prometheus::{Registry, Counter, CounterVec, Gauge, GaugeVec, Histogram, HistogramVec};

lazy_static\! {
    pub static ref REGISTRY: Registry = Registry::new();
    
    // Counters
    pub static ref EVENTS_DETECTED: CounterVec = register_counter_vec\!(
        "wa_events_detected_total",
        "Total events detected",
        &["type", "agent"]
    ).unwrap();
    
    pub static ref WORKFLOWS_COMPLETED: CounterVec = register_counter_vec\!(
        "wa_workflows_completed_total",
        "Total workflows completed",
        &["workflow", "status"]
    ).unwrap();
    
    pub static ref POLICY_DECISIONS: CounterVec = register_counter_vec\!(
        "wa_policy_decisions_total",
        "Total policy decisions",
        &["decision", "action"]
    ).unwrap();
    
    // Gauges
    pub static ref PANES_OBSERVED: Gauge = register_gauge\!(
        "wa_panes_observed",
        "Number of observed panes"
    ).unwrap();
    
    pub static ref QUEUE_DEPTH: Gauge = register_gauge\!(
        "wa_queue_depth",
        "Current event queue depth"
    ).unwrap();
    
    // Histograms
    pub static ref PATTERN_MATCH_DURATION: Histogram = register_histogram\!(
        "wa_pattern_match_duration_seconds",
        "Time to run pattern matching",
        vec\![0.0001, 0.0005, 0.001, 0.005, 0.01, 0.05, 0.1]
    ).unwrap();
}
```

### HTTP Endpoint
```rust
use axum::{Router, routing::get};
use prometheus::Encoder;

async fn metrics_handler() -> impl IntoResponse {
    let encoder = prometheus::TextEncoder::new();
    let metric_families = REGISTRY.gather();
    let mut buffer = Vec::new();
    encoder.encode(&metric_families, &mut buffer).unwrap();
    (
        [(header::CONTENT_TYPE, "text/plain; charset=utf-8")],
        buffer,
    )
}

pub fn metrics_router() -> Router {
    Router::new().route("/metrics", get(metrics_handler))
}
```

### Metric Instrumentation Points
- Event detection: increment counter, record duration
- Workflow execution: increment counter by status
- Policy decisions: increment by decision type
- Pane changes: update gauge
- Pattern matching: record histogram

## Metrics Catalog

| Metric | Type | Labels | Description |
|--------|------|--------|-------------|
| wa_events_detected_total | counter | type, agent | Events detected |
| wa_workflows_completed_total | counter | workflow, status | Workflow completions |
| wa_policy_decisions_total | counter | decision, action | Policy evaluations |
| wa_panes_observed | gauge | - | Active panes |
| wa_queue_depth | gauge | - | Pending events |
| wa_db_size_bytes | gauge | - | Database size |
| wa_pattern_match_duration_seconds | histogram | - | Pattern match time |
| wa_workflow_step_duration_seconds | histogram | workflow, step | Step execution time |

## Testing
- Unit: Metrics increment correctly
- Integration: Endpoint serves valid Prometheus format
- E2E: Metrics change during workflow execution

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All core operations instrumented
- [ ] /metrics endpoint serves Prometheus format
- [ ] Cardinality kept reasonable (bounded labels)
- [ ] Histograms have sensible buckets
- [ ] Feature-flagged for minimal deployments

 {"id":"wa-2toy3","title":"Phase 5: Refactor feature-gated modules (web, MCP, distributed, browser)","description":"# Phase 5: Refactor feature-gated modules

## Goal
Migrate all feature-gated async modules to asupersync. These are lower priority because they are optional and not always compiled.

## Modules

### web.rs (feature: web)
- FastAPI-based HTTP server on TcpListener
- Serves REST endpoints: /health, /panes, /events, /search
- Mutex/RwLock for request state
- Will need FastAPI replaced or ported (tracked separately)

### mcp.rs (feature: mcp)
- FastMCP-based MCP tool server
- Async tool handlers with mpsc response channels
- Mutex for tool registration
- Will need FastMCP replaced or ported (tracked separately)

### distributed.rs (feature: distributed)
- TLS/mTLS with tokio-rustls
- Certificate management
- Currently scaffold/stub — lower effort to migrate

### browser/*.rs (Anthropic/Google auth)
- Playwright-based browser automation
- Async flow execution with timeouts
- Channel-based event subscription
- These may need special handling for Playwright async compat

## Acceptance criteria
- All feature-gated modules build with asupersync
- Web server serves endpoints correctly
- MCP tools execute and respond
- TLS/mTLS handshake works
- Browser auth flows complete

  - `cargo check --features <combination>` succeeds
  - No dead code warnings from unused asupersync imports
  - No conflicting trait implementations between feature-gated modules
  - Runtime initialization works correctly regardless of which features are enabled
  - This is critical because feature flag interactions are a common source of compilation failures that only surface in CI.

## Benchmark requirements

  - web.rs: HTTP request handling latency (req → response) for /health, /panes endpoints
  - mcp.rs: MCP tool invocation round-trip time
  - distributed.rs: TLS handshake latency (client + server)
  - browser/*.rs: Auth flow step latency (page navigation, form fill, token extraction)

 {"id":"wa-2ufom","title":"Make Lua optional in FrankenTerm mux crate (feature-flag)","description":"# Make Lua Optional in FrankenTerm Mux Crate

## Context
The mux crate (frankenterm/mux/) depends on mlua and luahelper for Lua-scripted mux domain management and event callbacks. Under the dual-runtime strategy, these become optional.

## Dependencies to make optional
- mlua.workspace = true (currently line ~29)
- luahelper.workspace = true (currently line ~27)  
- termwiz-funcs.workspace = true (has Lua deps, needs feature-gating)

## Strategy
1. Gate mlua/luahelper as optional deps behind `lua` feature
2. All Lua callback code in mux/ gets `#[cfg(feature = "lua")]`
3. Default features include `lua` for backward compat
4. `no-lua` excludes it
5. The mux crate must function for pane management, tab management, domain handling WITHOUT Lua — Lua is only needed for event callbacks and scripted config

## Key files to modify
- frankenterm/mux/Cargo.toml (feature flags)
- frankenterm/mux/src/domain.rs (Lua domain callbacks)
- frankenterm/mux/src/tab.rs (any Lua hooks)
- Any file using `mlua::` or `luahelper::`

## Testing
- `cargo check -p mux --no-default-features` — must compile
- `cargo check -p mux` — must work (default, with Lua)
- `cargo test -p mux` — existing tests pass

## Relationship
- Depends on wa-ggw9w (config crate must be Lua-optional first, since mux depends on config)
- Blocks wa-3k2g1 (luahelper becomes unused when both config and mux have no-lua)

## Cross-References
- **wa-3k2g1** (luahelper optional): This bead and wa-ggw9w together make luahelper fully optional. Once both config and mux gate their luahelper deps behind `lua`, the luahelper crate compiles only when explicitly needed.
- **wa-ggw9w** (Config Lua optional): The config crate's Lua optionality must be done first (or in parallel), since mux depends on config. If config still hard-depends on Lua, mux cannot fully eliminate its Lua dependency chain.
- Together with wa-3k2g1 and wa-ggw9w, this forms the "Lua-optional triad" — all three must land for a clean no-Lua build path.","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-10T06:52:47.829473Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-10T19:44:28.681795Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-2ufom","depends_on_id":"wa-3dfxb","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T06:52:56.411066Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"},{"issue_id":"wa-2ufom","depends_on_id":"wa-ggw9w","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T06:52:56.528376Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"}]}
-{"id":"wa-2umk2","title":"[EPIC] FrankenTerm In-Tree Integration — Own WezTerm source, radical modifications enabled","description":"# FrankenTerm In-Tree Integration

## What This Is
Bring the full WezTerm source in-tree as owned FrankenTerm code. 30 crates (transitive closure of codec, config, mux, wezterm-term plus base91) are now committed, modifiable source under frankenterm/. This replaces the 4 git deps that previously pulled from github.com/wez/wezterm.

## Why "FrankenTerm"
The name reflects the project's philosophy: take the best pieces from different terminal projects (WezTerm's mux protocol, Zellij's layout system, Ghostty's performance innovations) and unify them into something greater than the sum of its parts. Like Frankenstein's monster — assembled from the best parts, then animated with new life.

## Strategic Goals
1. **Memory leak fixes**: WezTerm mux server leaks ~76GB RSS over 23 days under agent swarm load. In-tree ownership lets us patch root causes.
2. **Lua removal**: mlua + vendored LuaJIT add ~30s to clean builds and are a performance bottleneck. Replace with native Rust config/event system.
3. **asupersync migration**: Replace smol/tokio/async-io throughout FrankenTerm crates with asupersync for unified structured concurrency.
4. **Custom allocators**: jemalloc + per-pane arenas for predictable memory behavior under swarm load.
5. **Event hooks**: Native Rust event sink replacing Lua callback overhead.
6. **FrankenMux**: Hybrid muxer combining WezTerm's protocol with Zellij's layout innovations.
7. **Ghostty learnings**: Steal performance innovations from Ghostty (Zig) adapted to Rust.

## Scope
30 crates from WezTerm workspace (transitive closure of codec, config, mux, wezterm-term). Does NOT include GUI, font, window system, or server binary crates. Source commit: 05343b387085842b434d267f91b6b0ec157e4331.

## Architecture
- frankenterm/ = ex-WezTerm owned code (30 crates)
- crates/ = wa-authored code (wa, wa-core)
- legacy_wezterm/ = pristine upstream reference (gitignored)
- Feature flag: `frankenterm` (was `vendored`), backward compat `vendored` alias preserved

## Relationship to Existing Beads
- Supersedes bd-20fw (minimal fork strategy) and bd-1ez8 (nightly rebase CI)
- Unblocks wa-3kxe.1 (memory leak analysis — now possible on owned code)
- Updates wa-vv3h (rename operates on in-tree code)
- Feeds into wa-e34d9 (asupersync migration — now applies to frankenterm crates too)
- Feeds into bd-2oby (Lua elimination — now possible via source modification)

## Key Decisions
- thiserror: Kept 2.0.18 (our version), source-compatible with upstream 1.0 uses
- bitflags: Workspace 1.3 (upstream), crates needing 2.0 use direct version deps
- notify: 5.0.0 (upstream), no wa crate uses it directly
- criterion: 0.5 (upstream), wa-core uses own direct 0.7
- finl_unicode: Git dep from wez/finl_unicode fork (no_std branch)
- FrankenTerm crates do NOT inherit workspace lints (which forbid unsafe)
- Each crate keeps its original edition (2018/2021/2024)

## Verification (all pass)
- cargo check (default) — OK
- cargo check --features frankenterm — OK, all 30 crates compile
- cargo test -p wa-core (default) — 2815+ tests, 0 failures
- cargo test -p wa-core --features frankenterm — 2957+ tests, 0 failures
- cargo clippy --all — 0 errors

## Cross-References
- **wa-vv3h** (Rename epic): The rename epic operates on the in-tree code this bead provides. Directory and crate renames apply to the 30 frankenterm/ crates.
- **wa-3dfxb** (Scripting engine epic): The scripting engine replaces Lua in the in-tree config/mux crates. In-tree ownership is a prerequisite for modifying these crates to support the dual Lua+WASM runtime.
- **Meta-epic note**: This is the foundational meta-epic that enables ALL other FrankenTerm work. Without in-tree ownership, none of the fork hardening (wa-3kxe), scripting engine (wa-3dfxb), rename (wa-vv3h), asupersync migration (wa-brc7d), or performance optimization (wa-iehgn) epics can proceed. Every radical modification to WezTerm internals depends on this bead being completed first.","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-10T06:50:41.046919Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-10T19:42:21.750803Z"}

 {"id":"wa-2v4t","title":"Add alt-screen detection via escape sequence parsing in pattern engine","description":"## Overview

Implement alt-screen (alternate screen buffer) detection by parsing terminal escape sequences in the captured output stream, eliminating the need for Lua's \`pane:is_alt_screen_active()\`.

## Background

Terminal applications (vim, less, htop, etc.) switch to the alternate screen buffer using standardized escape sequences:

\`\`\`
Enter alt-screen: ESC [ ? 1049 h   (smcup - start mode cup)
Leave alt-screen: ESC [ ? 1049 l   (rmcup - reset mode cup)
\`\`\`

These sequences are emitted by the application and captured in wa's output stream. By detecting them, we know the alt-screen state without asking WezTerm via Lua.

## Technical Details

### Escape Sequence Variants
Some terminals/apps use alternative sequences:
\`\`\`
Primary (most common):
  ESC [ ? 1049 h    — enter
  ESC [ ? 1049 l    — leave

Alternative (xterm compatibility):
  ESC [ ? 47 h      — enter (older)
  ESC [ ? 47 l      — leave (older)

Combined with cursor save/restore:
  ESC [ ? 1049 h ESC [ ? 1 h   — enter + application cursor keys
\`\`\`

### Implementation Location

Add to **crates/wa-core/src/patterns.rs** or create new **crates/wa-core/src/screen_state.rs**:

\`\`\`rust
/// Tracks alt-screen state by parsing escape sequences
pub struct ScreenStateTracker {
    /// Current alt-screen state per pane
    alt_screen_active: HashMap<u64, bool>,
}

impl ScreenStateTracker {
    /// Process captured output, update alt-screen state
    pub fn process_output(&mut self, pane_id: u64, output: &[u8]) {
        // Scan for ESC [ ? 1049 h/l sequences
        // Update self.alt_screen_active[pane_id]
    }
    
    /// Query current alt-screen state
    pub fn is_alt_screen(&self, pane_id: u64) -> bool {
        self.alt_screen_active.get(&pane_id).copied().unwrap_or(false)
    }
}
\`\`\`

### Integration Points

1. **Ingest loop** (ingest.rs): After capturing pane output, pass to ScreenStateTracker
2. **Pattern detection** (patterns.rs): Can use alt-screen state in rule matching
3. **IPC server** (ipc.rs): StatusUpdate can report alt-screen from tracker

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] ScreenStateTracker struct implemented
- [ ] Detects ESC[?1049h as alt-screen enter
- [ ] Detects ESC[?1049l as alt-screen leave
- [ ] Handles sequences split across capture boundaries
- [ ] Unit tests with various escape sequence formats
- [ ] Integration with ingest loop
- [ ] Benchmark showing minimal overhead

## Test Cases

1. **Basic enter/leave**: Single ESC[?1049h followed by ESC[?1049l
2. **Partial sequence**: Sequence split across two output chunks
3. **Nested/repeated**: Multiple enters without leave (state stays active)
4. **Mixed content**: Normal text interspersed with sequences
5. **Alternative sequences**: Test ESC[?47h/l variants
6. **No sequences**: Normal output doesn't change state

## Performance Considerations

- Use memchr or Aho-Corasick for efficient ESC scanning
- Avoid regex for this (too slow for every output chunk)
- Track state change timestamps for debugging

## Files to Modify

- crates/wa-core/src/lib.rs — add mod screen_state
- NEW: crates/wa-core/src/screen_state.rs — ScreenStateTracker
- crates/wa-core/src/ingest.rs — integrate tracker
- crates/wa-core/src/ipc.rs — StatusUpdate uses tracker

## References

- XTerm control sequences: https://invisible-island.net/xterm/ctlseqs/ctlseqs.html
- ECMA-48 (ANSI escape codes)
- Existing pattern engine: crates/wa-core/src/patterns.rs","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-28T21:43:56.669479578Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-28T22:16:02.822658602Z","closed_at":"2026-01-28T22:16:02.822570318Z"}

 {"id":"wa-2wed","title":"[EPIC] FrankenTUI Adoption Program for wa (across-the-board migration)","description":"## Background
`wa` currently has a feature-gated TUI built around `ratatui`/`crossterm` (`crates/wa-core/src/tui`). We now want to standardize on `/dp/frankentui` (ftui) across the project’s interactive terminal surfaces.

FrankenTUI contributes a stricter kernel model:
- one-writer rule for terminal ownership
- deterministic render loop (buffer/diff/presenter)
- inline-first behavior for scrollback safety
- explicit operational quality gates (PTY E2E, snapshot and perf baselines)

## Program Goal
Migrate wa’s TUI architecture and operator workflows to ftui across the board, with a staged rollout that preserves behavior parity, improves determinism, and removes ratatui-centric technical debt.

## Scope
- dependency/build integration with `/dp/frankentui`
- runtime/output ownership model migration
- screen-by-screen UI migration
- command/input parity migration
- testing/perf/compatibility gates
- documentation, rollout, and old-stack decommissioning plan

## Non-Goals
- No broad rewrite of robot/MCP/storage logic unrelated to UI migration.
- No speculative feature expansion before parity and reliability gates are met.

## Success Criteria
- wa TUI experience runs on ftui with feature parity (or explicit intentional deltas).
- deterministic tests and PTY E2E prove stability under sustained output and resize/input stress.

-{"id":"wa-6js","title":"CLI command timeout hardening — kill_on_drop and orphan reaper","description":"# CLI Command Timeout Hardening — kill_on_drop and Orphan Reaper

## Goal
Add aggressive timeout enforcement and orphan process cleanup to wa CLI subprocess spawning, preventing stuck wezterm cli process accumulation.

## Background
WezTerm CLI commands can hang indefinitely due to lock contention, no socket read timeout, notification feedback loops. We observed 60+ stuck processes on production servers.

## kill_on_drop Wrapper
Modify run_cli() in wezterm.rs:
- tokio::Command with kill_on_drop(true)
- Enforce configurable timeout (default 15s)
- On timeout: child is dropped → killed → Error::CliTimeout returned

## Orphan Reaper
Periodic background task in wa watch:
- Scan for wezterm cli processes older than threshold (default 30s)
- **Linux**: pgrep + /proc/<pid>/stat for process age
- **macOS**: pgrep + ps -o etime= for process age
- Kill orphans with SIGKILL after age threshold
- Log reap report: scanned count, killed count

## Configuration
```toml
[cli]
timeout_seconds = 15
orphan_reap_interval = 60
orphan_age_seconds = 30
```

## Tests
- CLI command respects timeout (mock slow command)
- kill_on_drop cleanly terminates timed-out processes
- Orphan reaper identifies and kills old processes
- Reap report logged with correct counts
- No FD or resource leaks from killed processes
- **Test on both Linux and macOS** (process age detection differs)
- **Criterion benchmarks**: reaper scan <10ms for 1000 processes

## Acceptance criteria
- CLI commands timeout after configured seconds
- kill_on_drop ensures cleanup even on wa crash/panic
- Orphan reaper runs periodically
- Works correctly on Linux and macOS
- No resource leaks from killed processes","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-02-09T19:19:32.031847Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-11T01:19:38.656728Z"}

 {"id":"wa-6sk","title":"[EPIC] Event Timeline with Cross-Pane Correlation","description":"# [EPIC] Event Timeline with Cross-Pane Correlation

## Mission
Provide a **unified temporal view** of events across all panes, with correlation highlighting to show relationships between events.

## Why This Matters
When managing agent swarms, the question is often: "What happened across all my agents?"

Individual event logs are:
- Per-pane (hard to see the big picture)
- Chronologically disjoint
- Missing correlation context

A unified timeline shows:
- All events on a shared time axis
- Correlations across panes (failover triggered by usage limit)
- Patterns over time (compaction always happens 10 min in)

## How It Works

### CLI Mode
```bash
$ wa timeline --last 30m

Timeline: 2026-01-18 14:00 - 14:30 (4 panes, 12 events)

14:02:15 ─┬─ Pane 1 (codex): session.started
          │
14:05:32 ─┼─ Pane 3 (claude): session.started  
          │
14:12:01 ─┼─ Pane 1 (codex): session.compaction ──→ handled (14:12:08)
          │
14:28:03 ─┼─ Pane 1 (codex): codex.usage_limit_reached
          ├──→ workflow: handle_usage_limits (in progress)
          │
14:28:45 ─┴─ Pane 7 (codex-backup): session.started [CORRELATED: failover]

Legend: ──→ workflow action  [CORRELATED] cross-pane relationship
```

### TUI Mode
Interactive scrollable timeline with:
- Zoom in/out on time ranges
- Click events to see details
- Filter by pane, event type, severity
- Correlation lines connecting related events

### Robot Mode
```json
{
  "timeline": {
    "start": "2026-01-18T14:00:00Z",
    "end": "2026-01-18T14:30:00Z",
    "events": [...],
    "correlations": [
      {"events": [123, 456], "type": "failover", "confidence": 0.95}
    ]
  }
}
```

## Correlation Detection
Simple heuristics for v1:
- **Temporal**: Events within N seconds
- **Causal**: Usage limit → failover session start
- **Agent-related**: Same agent type, different panes
- **Workflow-related**: Events handled by same workflow run

## Key Design Decisions
- Timeline is **read-only** (query, not mutation)
- Correlations are **heuristic** (may be wrong, show confidence)
- Timeline is **bounded** (default last 1h, configurable)
- Output supports **JSON** for automation

## Testing
- Unit tests: correlation detection produces expected results
- Integration tests: timeline aggregates events correctly
- Visual tests: ASCII output renders correctly for various scenarios

## Success Criteria
- `wa timeline` shows events across all panes on unified axis
- Correlations are highlighted with relationship type
- TUI timeline is interactive and responsive
- Timeline query is fast (<100ms for typical time ranges)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Timeline aggregates events across panes with correlation markers.
- wa timeline produces a stable ASCII view.
- TUI timeline view works when enabled.
- wa-6sk.5 tests pass.

 {"id":"wa-6sk.1","title":"Timeline data model: unified event view with cross-pane aggregation","description":"
# Timeline Data Model

## Purpose
Define the data structures and queries for aggregating events across panes into a unified timeline.

## Data Structures
```rust
pub struct TimelineEvent {
    pub id: EventId,
    pub timestamp: DateTime<Utc>,
    pub pane_id: PaneId,
    pub pane_info: PaneInfo,  // agent type, domain, cwd
    pub event_type: String,
    pub severity: Severity,
    pub handled: Option<HandledInfo>,
    pub correlations: Vec<CorrelationRef>,
}

pub struct Timeline {
    pub start: DateTime<Utc>,
    pub end: DateTime<Utc>,
    pub events: Vec<TimelineEvent>,
    pub correlations: Vec<Correlation>,
}

pub struct Correlation {
    pub id: CorrelationId,
    pub event_ids: Vec<EventId>,
    pub correlation_type: CorrelationType,
    pub confidence: f32,
    pub description: String,
}

pub enum CorrelationType {
    Failover,     // Usage limit → new session
    Cascade,      // One event triggers another
    Temporal,     // Close in time
    WorkflowGroup, // Same workflow run
}
```

## Query API
```rust
pub async fn get_timeline(
    range: TimeRange,
    filters: TimelineFilters,
) -> Result<Timeline>;
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] TimelineEvent struct captures all needed info
- [ ] Timeline aggregates events from all panes
- [ ] Query is efficient (indexed by timestamp)
- [ ] Pagination for large time ranges

## Testing
- Unit tests for correlation heuristics and aggregation.
- Integration tests for timeline queries and rendering.
- E2E scenario verifies correlations with artifacts.

 {"id":"wa-y2e","title":"Robot mode dry-run: wa robot … --dry-run previews (send/workflow/events)","description":"# Robot mode dry-run previews (`wa robot … --dry-run`)

## Goal
Extend dry-run preview semantics to robot-mode commands so an AI agent can plan safely before taking actions.

This task is specifically about **robot mode** (machine output), and complements the **human** dry-run work in `wa-1pe.*`.

## Scope
Add `--dry-run` to robot commands that would otherwise mutate state:
- `wa robot send --dry-run …`
- `wa robot workflow … --dry-run`

Also add “planning previews” for read-only commands that *cause the agent to act*:
- `wa robot events --would-handle --dry-run` (preview which workflows would run, without running them)

Non-goals (for v0.1):
- actually granting approvals from robot mode (approval is intentionally a human/interactive surface)

## Output contract
- Uses the standard robot envelope (`wa-4vx.7.1`, schemas in `wa-4vx.7.10`).
- Must be deterministic and redacted.
- Must never:
  - send text
  - acquire locks/reservations
  - mutate DB state (other than unavoidable read-side effects like opening the DB)

## Examples

### `wa robot send --dry-run`
```json
{
  "ok": true,
  "data": {
    "command": "send",
    "dry_run": true,
    "target": {
      "pane_id": 3,
      "title": "codex @ /project",
      "agent_type": "codex"
    },
    "input": {
      "text_preview": "continue",
      "text_len": 8
    },
    "policy_evaluation": {
      "decision": "allow",
      "capabilities": {
        "prompt_active": true,
        "command_running": false,
        "alt_screen": false,
        "recent_gap": false
      },
      "command_safety": {
        "is_command_candidate": false,
        "decision": "allow"
      }
    },
    "would_execute": true
  },
  "error": null
}
```

### `wa robot workflow run --dry-run`
```json
{
  "ok": true,
  "data": {
    "command": "workflow_run",
    "dry_run": true,
    "workflow": "handle_compaction",
    "target": {"pane_id": 3, "title": "claude @ /project"},
    "steps_preview": [
      {"step": 1, "name": "verify_compaction_state", "kind": "check_pattern"},
      {"step": 2, "name": "inject_context", "kind": "send_text", "text_preview": "Please re-read …"},
      {"step": 3, "name": "wait_ack", "kind": "wait_for", "pattern": "I've reviewed"}
    ],
    "estimated_duration_ms": 30000
  },
  "error": null
}
```

### `wa robot events --would-handle --dry-run`
```json
{
  "ok": true,
  "data": {
    "command": "events",
    "dry_run": true,
    "events": [
      {
        "event_id": 123,
        "rule_id": "codex.usage_limit_reached",
        "pane_id": 9,
        "would_handle_with": {
          "workflow": "handle_usage_limits",
          "first_step": "exit_codex",
          "estimated_duration_ms": 60000
        }
      }
    ]
  },
  "error": null
}
```

## Design notes
- Robot dry-run should reuse the same planning logic as human dry-run where possible:
  - shared `DryRunReport` structs (different renderers)
  - same PolicyEngine evaluation paths
- If `--dry-run` is combined with `--wait-for` style arguments, the preview should include:
  - what matcher would be used
  - timeout/backoff parameters
  - but must not actually wait

## Testing
- Unit/contract tests:
  - `--dry-run` never calls SendText / workflow execution
  - outputs validate against JSON schemas (`wa-4vx.7.10`)
  - redaction invariants hold (no secrets in previews)

- E2E:
  - add an adapter case to the E2E registry (`wa-4vx.10.20`) that runs:
    - `wa robot send --dry-run …`
    - `wa robot workflow … --dry-run`
    - `wa robot events --would-handle --dry-run`
  - assert:
    - outputs are parseable JSON
    - `dry_run: true`
    - no audit “action executed” rows were created

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `wa robot send --dry-run` returns a deterministic, schema-valid preview including policy evaluation.
- [ ] `wa robot workflow … --dry-run` returns a deterministic, schema-valid step preview.
- [ ] `wa robot events --would-handle --dry-run` previews which workflows would run.
- [ ] Dry-run paths do not acquire locks or mutate panes/DB state.

 {"id":"wa-y59","title":"Secure file permissions: restrictive umask for DB, logs, sockets","description":"# Task: Secure File Permissions

## Goal
Ensure wa files are created with appropriate permissions to prevent unauthorized access.

## Files Requiring Protection
1. **Database (wa.db)**: Contains terminal transcripts - may include secrets typed by user
2. **Logs**: May contain debugging info that includes sensitive paths/commands
3. **IPC sockets**: Used for inter-process communication
4. **Config files**: May contain webhook URLs with tokens
5. **Backup files**: Contains all above

## Implementation

### Permission Requirements
| File Type | Unix Mode | Rationale |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|
| Database | 0600 | Owner read/write only |
| Logs | 0600 | Owner read/write only |
| IPC socket | 0600 | Owner only |
| Lock files | 0644 | Others can check existence |
| Config | 0600 | May contain secrets |
| .wa directory | 0700 | Owner traverse only |

### Implementation
```rust
use std::os::unix::fs::PermissionsExt;

pub fn create_secure_file(path: &Path) -> io::Result<File> {
    let file = File::create(path)?;
    let mut perms = file.metadata()?.permissions();
    perms.set_mode(0o600);
    file.set_permissions(perms)?;
    Ok(file)
}

pub fn create_secure_directory(path: &Path) -> io::Result<()> {
    fs::create_dir_all(path)?;
    let mut perms = fs::metadata(path)?.permissions();
    perms.set_mode(0o700);
    fs::set_permissions(path, perms)?;
    Ok(())
}
```

### Startup Validation
On startup, check existing file permissions and warn if too permissive:
```
WARNING: Database permissions too open (0644)
  File: /home/user/.wa/wa.db
  Recommended: chmod 600 /home/user/.wa/wa.db
```

## Testing
- Unit tests: file creation with correct permissions
- Integration: wa doctor checks permissions
- E2E: new installation creates secure files

## Acceptance Criteria
- All sensitive files created with restrictive permissions
- wa doctor warns about insecure permissions
- Documentation explains security model","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-18T19:59:49.464206636Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-22T04:31:47.04551877Z","closed_at":"2026-01-22T04:31:47.045439511Z","close_reason":"Implementation verified complete: Secure file permissions throughout codebase. storage.rs:977-1001 - set_permissions() creates DB/WAL/SHM files with 0o600, parent dirs with 0o700. logging.rs:119-161 - set_file_permissions() for log files at 0o600, log dirs at 0o700. config.rs:1662-1692 - check_permission() in wa doctor validates all sensitive paths (workspace dir, logs dir, crash dir, db, log files, ipc socket, config).  {"id":"wa-y6g","title":"Schema migration framework: versioned migrations with rollback support","description":"# Task: Schema Migration Framework

## Goal
Enable wa to safely upgrade its database schema across versions without data loss.

## Why This Matters
As wa evolves:
- New columns/tables needed for features
- Indexes may change for performance
- Data format may evolve

Without migrations, users would need to:
- Delete database and lose history
- Manually transform data

## Design

### Migration Files
```
src/storage/migrations/
├── mod.rs
├── v001_initial_schema.rs
├── v002_add_event_metadata.rs
├── v003_fts_improvements.rs
└── v004_workflow_state.rs
```

### Migration Structure
```rust
pub struct Migration {
    pub version: u32,
    pub name: &'static str,
    pub up: fn(&Connection) -> Result<()>,
    pub down: Option<fn(&Connection) -> Result<()>>,
}

// Example migration
pub static V002_ADD_EVENT_METADATA: Migration = Migration {
    version: 2,
    name: "add_event_metadata",
    up: |conn| {
        conn.execute_batch("
            ALTER TABLE events ADD COLUMN metadata TEXT;
            CREATE INDEX idx_events_metadata ON events(json_extract(metadata, '$.type'));
        ")?;
        Ok(())
    },
    down: Some(|conn| {
        conn.execute_batch("
            DROP INDEX IF EXISTS idx_events_metadata;
            ALTER TABLE events DROP COLUMN metadata;
        ")?;
        Ok(())
    }),
};
```

### Migration Runner
```rust
pub struct Migrator {
    migrations: Vec<&'static Migration>,
}

impl Migrator {
    pub fn run(&self, conn: &Connection) -> Result<MigrationReport> {
        let current = self.get_current_version(conn)?;
        let target = self.latest_version();
        
        tracing::info\!(
            current_version = current,
            target_version = target,
            "Starting migration"
        );
        
        if current == target {
            return Ok(MigrationReport::AlreadyCurrent);
        }
        
        // Run migrations in transaction
        conn.execute("BEGIN EXCLUSIVE", [])?;
        
        for migration in &self.migrations {
            if migration.version > current {
                tracing::info\!(
                    version = migration.version,
                    name = migration.name,
                    "Running migration"
                );
                
                (migration.up)(conn)?;
                self.record_migration(conn, migration)?;
            }
        }
        
        conn.execute("COMMIT", [])?;
        
        Ok(MigrationReport::Migrated {
            from: current,
            to: target,
        })
    }
}
```

### CLI Integration
```bash
# Automatic on startup (default)
$ wa watch
Migrating database: v1 → v3
  Running: v002_add_event_metadata... OK
  Running: v003_fts_improvements... OK
Migration complete.

# Manual migration
$ wa db migrate
Current version: 1
Target version: 3
Run migrations? [y/N]

# Check status without migrating
$ wa db migrate --status
Current version: 1
Available migrations:
  ✓ v001_initial_schema (applied)
  ○ v002_add_event_metadata (pending)
  ○ v003_fts_improvements (pending)
```

## Safety
- All migrations run in transaction
- Backup created before migration (if large changes)
- Down migrations available for rollback
- Version tracked in dedicated table

## Testing
- Unit tests: each migration up/down
- Integration: migrate from v1 to vN
- E2E: upgrade simulation with real data

## Acceptance Criteria
- Migrations run automatically on startup
- Failed migration rolls back cleanly
- Migration status visible via CLI
- Down migrations available for emergencies

-{"id":"wa-ybq","title":"Scrollback capture engine via vendored mux client","description":"## Goal
Implement efficient scrollback capture for all panes using wa's existing vendored mux client, bypassing the CLI overhead of wezterm cli get-text.

## Background
wa already has a vendored mux client at crates/wa-core/src/vendored/mux_client.rs that connects directly to the WezTerm mux server via Unix socket. This is faster than spawning wezterm cli processes. The mux client already supports ListPanes and GetLines PDUs.

For session persistence, we need to capture the full scrollback of every pane (not just the viewport). WezTerm's get_lines_as_escapes() preserves ANSI color/style sequences which we need for faithful restoration.

## Design
1. Extend the vendored mux client to support:
   - get_lines(pane_id, first_row, last_row) -- fetch lines with escape sequences

 {"id":"wa-ye8","title":"Enriched error messages: every error includes actionable remediation","description":"# Task: Enriched Error Messages

## Goal
Transform all wa error messages to include **actionable remediation steps**. Errors become guides, not dead ends.

## Why This Matters
Cryptic errors waste user time and create frustration:
- "Error: DB locked" → What do I do?
- "Error: Pane not found" → Which panes exist?
- "Error: Send denied" → Why? How do I fix it?

Enriched errors answer the "now what?" question immediately.

## Error Message Standard
Every error must include:
1. **What happened**: Clear description
2. **Why it happened**: Context/cause (when knowable)
3. **How to fix it**: Specific, copy-pasteable commands (prefer safe commands)
4. **Learn more**: Link to docs or `wa why` command

## Examples

### Before
```
Error: DB locked
```

### After
```
Error: Cannot start watcher - workspace lock is held

Another wa process may already be running.

To fix:
  - See who is running it: wa doctor
  - Stop the watcher safely: wa stop
  - If the lock appears stale, follow wa doctor's instructions (do not guess)

Learn more:
  - wa why (for decision/audit explanations)
  - wa doctor (for environment + workspace paths)
```

### Before
```
Error: Pane 99 not found
```

### After
```
Error: Pane 99 not found

Available panes:
  1: codex @ /home/user/project
  3: claude @ /home/user/other
  7: gemini @ /home/user/third

Did you mean: wa send --pane 9 "hello" (closest match)

To list all panes: wa status
```

### Before
```
Error: Send denied
```

### After
```
Error: Send denied - pane is in AltScreen mode

Pane 3 (claude_code) is currently running a full-screen application.
Sending input now could corrupt the application state.

To fix:
  - Wait for the application to exit
  - Check pane state: wa status --pane 3

To understand:
  - wa why denied --pane 3

To proceed if you truly must override:
  - use the allow-once approval flow (wa will provide a short code)
  - run: wa approve <code>
  - retry the original action
```

## Implementation

### 1. Error Type with Remediation
```rust
#[derive(Debug, thiserror::Error)]
pub enum WaError {
    #[error("Cannot start watcher - workspace lock is held")]
    WorkspaceLockHeld {
        lock_path: PathBuf,
        remediation: Vec<Remediation>,
    },

    #[error("Pane {pane_id} not found")]
    PaneNotFound {
        pane_id: PaneId,
        available_panes: Vec<PaneInfo>,
        closest_match: Option<PaneId>,
        remediation: Vec<Remediation>,
    },
    // ... more variants
}

pub enum Remediation {
    Command { description: &'static str, command: String },
    Url { description: &'static str, url: &'static str },
    Hint { text: String },
}
```

### 2. Error Formatting
```rust
impl WaError {
    pub fn display_rich(&self) -> String {
        let mut output = String::new();
        output.push_str(&format!("Error: {}\n\n", self));

        if let Some(context) = self.context() {
            output.push_str(&format!("{}\n\n", context));
        }

        output.push_str("To fix:\n");
        for remedy in self.remediation() {
            output.push_str(&format!("  - {}\n", remedy));
        }

        output
    }
}
```

### 3. Context-Aware Remediation
Use runtime context to generate specific commands:
- include actual paths, pane IDs, timestamps
- detect platform for OS-specific commands
- show closest matches for typos

### 4. Integration Points
- CLI error handler uses `display_rich()`
- Robot mode errors include remediation in JSON
- MCP errors include remediation hints

## Testing
- Unit tests: Each error type has valid remediation
- Integration tests: Errors include context-specific values
- Snapshot tests: Error messages match expected format
- UX tests: Remediation steps actually work

## Acceptance Criteria
- All error messages include "To fix:" with specific steps
- Context-specific values (paths, IDs) are included
- Remediation commands can be copy-pasted directly
- Error messages are consistent in format

 {"id":"wa-z0e","title":"[EPIC] Session Recording and Replay: capture, store, and replay agent sessions","description":"# [EPIC] Session Recording and Replay

## Mission
Enable users to record complete terminal sessions, replay them for analysis/debugging, and share them for collaboration/documentation.

## Why This Matters
Current state: wa captures segments and events, but users cannot:
- See a full session from start to finish
- Replay what happened to understand issues
- Share a recording with others
- Use recordings for demos or documentation

Session recording solves all these:
- **Debugging**: "What exactly happened before the crash?"
- **Collaboration**: "Here's a recording of the issue"
- **Documentation**: "Watch this recording to learn the workflow"
- **Compliance**: "Full audit trail of agent actions"

## Core Insight: Time-Travel Debugging
Recordings are like git for terminal sessions—you can move backward and forward through time, seeing exactly what happened at each moment.

## Scope

### Recording
- Start/stop recording for specific panes
- Automatic recording based on rules (e.g., "record all Codex panes")
- Metadata capture (timestamps, pane info, agent type)
- Efficient storage (delta-compressed)

### Replay
- Terminal-accurate playback at configurable speed
- Pause, seek, speed control
- Event annotations shown during replay
- Workflow step markers

### Export
- Self-contained recording files (.war = wa recording)
- Shareable without wa installed (HTML player option)
- Privacy controls (redact sensitive content)

### Integration
- `wa record start --pane 0`
- `wa record stop`
- `wa replay session-123.war`
- `wa replay --speed 2x`
- TUI replay viewer

## Success Criteria
- Recording adds <5% overhead to capture
- Replay is terminal-accurate (escape sequences preserved)
- Recordings can be shared and played back
- Users report recordings help with debugging

## Testing Requirements
- Unit tests for delta compression
- Integration tests for record/replay roundtrip
- E2E tests for full workflow recording
- Fuzz tests for malformed recording files

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Recording start/stop commands functional
- [ ] Recordings stored efficiently (delta-compressed)
- [ ] Replay command plays back accurately
- [ ] Speed control works (0.5x, 1x, 2x, 4x)
- [ ] Event annotations visible during replay
- [ ] Export to shareable format works
- [ ] Privacy redaction configurable
- [ ] Tests cover all functionality","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-01-18T17:48:22.423132786Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-02-08T20:46:45.975377588Z","closed_at":"2026-02-08T20:46:45.975311194Z","close_reason":"All child beads completed; closing stale-open epic to keep active plan set non-duplicative."}
